title: Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences Regulations 2013
jurisdiction: Great Britain (separate regulations for Northern Ireland: RIDDOR (NI) 1997)
chapters: SI 2013/1471. 14 Regulations + 5 Schedules. Made under HSWA 1974 ss.15(1), 15(2), 15(3)(a), 15(3)(c), 15(5)(b), 15(6)(d), 15(9), 80(1), 82(3)(a). Replaced RIDDOR 1995 (SI 1995/3163). The Regulations impose a mandatory obligation on employers and other 'responsible persons' to REPORT specified workplace incidents to the HSE (or local authority enforcing authority) and to KEEP RECORDS of those incidents for at least 3 years. Failure to report is a CRIMINAL OFFENCE.

parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Made by the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions under the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Replaced RIDDOR 1995 from 1 October 2013. The 2013 Regulations streamlined and simplified the reporting requirements: the list of reportable diseases and dangerous occurrences was shortened, and the reporting process was modernised (online reporting via HSE website). The over-3-day incapacitation reporting threshold had already been increased to over-7-days from 6 April 2012.

significantAmendments.instrument: Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences (Amendment) Regulations 2012 (SI 2012/199)
significantAmendments.effect: Increased the incapacitation reporting threshold from over 3 consecutive days to over 7 consecutive days (not including the day of the accident). This reduced the reporting burden on employers. Over-3-day incapacitations must still be RECORDED in the accident book but no longer reported to the HSE.

precedingDocuments.title: Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 (parent Act)
precedingDocuments.significance: RIDDOR is made under HSWA 1974. The general duty under HSWA s.2 to ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health, safety, and welfare of employees creates the foundation upon which RIDDOR reporting sits.

precedingDocuments.title: RIDDOR 1995 (SI 1995/3163) (replaced)
precedingDocuments.significance: The predecessor regulations which had broader reporting requirements including over-3-day injuries, a longer list of reportable diseases, and more specified dangerous occurrences. RIDDOR 2013 consolidated and simplified the framework.

precedingDocuments.title: Social Security (Claims and Payments) Regulations 1979
precedingDocuments.significance: Require employers to keep an ACCIDENT BOOK (Form BI 510). The accident book requirement is separate from RIDDOR but complementary — all accidents should be recorded in the book, and those that meet RIDDOR thresholds must additionally be reported to the HSE.

leadingCases.name: No leading higher court case law on RIDDOR reporting obligations
leadingCases.principle: RIDDOR enforcement is primarily through HSE prosecution in the magistrates' court or Crown Court. The most common prosecution scenario is an employer who fails to report a death, specified injury, or dangerous occurrence — which is then discovered by the HSE through other means (e.g., hospital notification, employee complaint, media coverage). Failure to report is treated seriously because it indicates either ignorance of the law or deliberate concealment.
leadingCases.significance: RIDDOR non-reporting is frequently an aggravating factor in HSE prosecutions for substantive health and safety offences. An employer who both causes an injury AND fails to report it faces more severe sentencing.

parts.title: Part 1 — What Must Be Reported and By Whom

sections.title: The five categories of reportable incident and the 'responsible person'
sections.summary: RIDDOR requires the reporting of FIVE categories of incident: (1) DEATHS — any person dying as a result of a work-related accident. (2) SPECIFIED INJURIES to workers — a defined list of serious injuries including fractures, amputations, crush injuries, burns covering more than 10% of the body, loss of consciousness, and hypothermia/heat illness from working in enclosed spaces. (3) OVER-7-DAY INCAPACITATION of workers — where a worker is incapacitated for more than 7 consecutive days (not including the day of the accident) and cannot perform the full range of their normal duties. (4) INJURIES TO NON-WORKERS (members of the public, visitors, customers) — where a person not at work is injured and taken directly to hospital from the scene. (5) OCCUPATIONAL DISEASES — specified diseases diagnosed by a doctor where the worker's employment is the likely cause. (6) DANGEROUS OCCURRENCES — specified near-miss events where no injury occurred but could have. (7) GAS INCIDENTS — reported by gas fitters. The 'responsible person' who must make the report is: the employer (for employees), the person in control of the premises (for self-employed and public), or the gas fitter (for gas incidents).
sections.text:
CATEGORY 1 — DEATHS (Reg.6(1)):
— Any person (worker or non-worker) dying as a result of a work-related accident.
— Must be reported immediately by the quickest practicable means (telephone to HSE: 0345 300 9923).
— Written report within 10 days via online form.
— If a worker dies within 1 year of a reportable accident, the death must also be reported.
— Suicides are NOT reportable under RIDDOR.

CATEGORY 2 — SPECIFIED INJURIES TO WORKERS (Reg.4 + Schedule 1):
The full list of specified injuries:
(a) Fractures — other than to fingers, thumbs, and toes.
(b) Amputation of an arm, hand, finger, thumb, leg, foot, or toe.
(c) Any injury likely to lead to permanent loss of sight or reduction in sight in one or both eyes.
(d) Any crush injury to the head or torso, causing damage to the brain or internal organs.
(e) Any burn injury (including scalding) covering more than 10% of the body surface, or damaging the eyes, respiratory system, or other vital organs.
(f) Any degree of scalping requiring hospital treatment.
(g) Loss of consciousness caused by head injury or asphyxia.
(h) Any other injury arising from working in an enclosed space which leads to hypothermia, heat-induced illness, or requires resuscitation or admittance to hospital for more than 24 hours.

Must be reported immediately (telephone for deaths/specified injuries), confirmed by written online report within 10 days.

CATEGORY 3 — OVER-7-DAY INCAPACITATION (Reg.4(2)):
— A worker is incapacitated for MORE THAN 7 consecutive days (not including the day of the accident).
— 'Incapacitated' means unable to perform the full range of their normal work duties.
— 'More than 7 consecutive days' means at least 8 days. The count includes weekends and rest days.
— Must be reported within 15 DAYS of the accident.
— Note: over-3-day incapacitations do NOT need to be reported to the HSE but MUST be recorded in the accident book.

CATEGORY 4 — INJURIES TO NON-WORKERS (Reg.5):
— A person not at work (visitor, customer, member of the public) is injured as a result of a work-related accident AND is taken directly from the scene of the accident to a hospital for treatment.
— No report required if the person declines help at the scene and later goes to hospital themselves.

CATEGORY 5 — OCCUPATIONAL DISEASES (Regs.8-9 + Schedule 3):
— A doctor diagnoses a specified occupational disease AND the worker's occupation is listed as a corresponding occupation for that disease.
— Examples: carpal tunnel syndrome (work involving regular use of vibrating tools), occupational dermatitis (work involving exposure to irritants/sensitisers), occupational asthma (work involving exposure to specified respiratory sensitisers), tendonitis/tenosynovitis (work involving frequent/repetitive movements), hand-arm vibration syndrome (work involving vibrating tools).

CATEGORY 6 — DANGEROUS OCCURRENCES (Reg.7 + Schedule 2):
— Specified near-miss events where no injury occurred but could have.
— 27 categories for most workplaces including: collapse/overturning of lifting equipment, explosion or fire caused by electrical short circuit, accidental release of a biological agent, collapse of scaffolding, dangerous gas incidents, unintended collapse of a building.

THE 'RESPONSIBLE PERSON' (Reg.2):
(a) For employees: the EMPLOYER.
(b) For self-employed persons and non-workers: the person in CONTROL OF THE PREMISES.
(c) For gas incidents: the registered GAS FITTER.
sections.keyPrinciple: RIDDOR reporting is MANDATORY, TIME-SENSITIVE, and CRIMINAL. Failure to report is a criminal offence under HSWA 1974 s.33. The HR manager must ensure that: (a) a clear internal reporting system exists so that ALL workplace incidents are assessed against RIDDOR criteria, (b) the 'responsible person' is identified and trained, (c) reports are made within the statutory deadlines (immediately for deaths/specified injuries, 15 days for over-7-day injuries), and (d) records are kept for at least 3 years.
refs: RIDDOR 2013 Schedule 1 (specified injuries — full list)
RIDDOR 2013 Schedule 2 (dangerous occurrences — 27 categories)
RIDDOR 2013 Schedule 3 (reportable diseases)
HSWA 1974 s.33 (criminal offence of failing to comply with regulations)
HSE online reporting form (www.hse.gov.uk/riddor/report.htm)
HSE Incident Contact Centre: 0345 300 9923 (deaths and specified injuries)

regulatoryGuidance.title: RIDDOR — Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences Regulations
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The HSE's primary RIDDOR guidance page with links to the online reporting forms, guidance on what is reportable, and frequently asked questions.

regulatoryGuidance.title: A brief guide to RIDDOR (INDG453)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Short practical guide for employers on RIDDOR reporting requirements.

commonErrors: Not reporting specified injuries because the employee 'seems fine' — the test is the nature of the injury, not the employee's subjective experience. A fracture is reportable even if the employee says they are not in pain
Counting the day of the accident when calculating the 7-day incapacitation period — the day of the accident is EXCLUDED. The 7-day count starts from the day AFTER the accident

Only counting working days in the 7-day period — weekends and rest days ARE included in the 7 consecutive days. An employee injured on Friday who cannot work the following Friday has been incapacitated for 7 consecutive days (Sat-Fri = 7 days)

Not reporting injuries to non-workers who are taken to hospital — if a customer, visitor, or member of the public is injured by a work-related accident and taken directly to hospital from the scene, this is reportable

Failing to report dangerous occurrences (near-misses) — RIDDOR covers events where no injury occurred but could have. Collapse of scaffolding, electrical fires, unintended releases of substances — all reportable even if nobody was hurt

Not knowing who the 'responsible person' is — in many organisations, nobody has been formally designated as the responsible person for RIDDOR reporting. The HR manager should ensure this is assigned, trained, and documented

Assuming that the accident book satisfies RIDDOR — the accident book is a separate legal requirement. Recording an incident in the accident book does NOT constitute a RIDDOR report. The RIDDOR report must be made separately via the HSE online form or telephone

parts.title: Part 2 — How and When to Report

sections.title: Reporting methods, deadlines, record-keeping, and the HR manager's response protocol
sections.summary: RIDDOR reports must be made to the HSE (or local authority enforcing authority) via specified methods within strict deadlines. The reporting method and deadline depend on the severity of the incident. Records must be kept for at least 3 years. The HR manager must establish a clear internal protocol: incident occurs → first aid → scene preservation → assess against RIDDOR criteria → report if required → investigate → record → implement corrective actions.
sections.text:
REPORTING METHODS:
(1) ONLINE: via the HSE online reporting form at www.hse.gov.uk/riddor/report.htm — available for ALL reportable incidents.
(2) TELEPHONE: 0345 300 9923 (HSE Incident Contact Centre) — for DEATHS and SPECIFIED INJURIES ONLY. Available Monday-Friday 08:30-17:00.
(3) Post and fax reporting have been DISCONTINUED.

DEADLINES:
(a) Deaths: report IMMEDIATELY by telephone. Written online report within 10 days.
(b) Specified injuries: report IMMEDIATELY by telephone or online. Written online report within 10 days.
(c) Over-7-day incapacitation: report within 15 DAYS of the accident.
(d) Occupational diseases: report as soon as the diagnosis is received.
(e) Dangerous occurrences: report IMMEDIATELY by telephone or online. Written online report within 10 days.
(f) Gas incidents: report as soon as the incident is discovered.

RECORD-KEEPING (Reg.12 + Schedule 4):
(1) The responsible person must keep a record of EVERY reportable incident.
(2) A printed/saved copy of the online RIDDOR report form satisfies this requirement.
(3) Records must be kept for AT LEAST 3 YEARS from the date of the incident.
(4) Records must include: date and time of accident, details of the injured person, nature of injury/disease, a brief description of the circumstances, the place where the incident occurred.
(5) Records must be produced on request to an HSE inspector.

HR MANAGER'S RESPONSE PROTOCOL:

STEP 1 — IMMEDIATE RESPONSE:
(a) Ensure the injured person receives first aid and medical attention.
(b) Make the area safe to prevent further injury.
(c) Preserve the scene for investigation (do not clean up or move equipment until the scene has been assessed).

STEP 2 — ASSESS AGAINST RIDDOR CRITERIA:
(a) Is the incident work-related? (Would it have happened if the person had not been at work?)
(b) Does it fall into one of the RIDDOR categories? (Death, specified injury, over-7-day, non-worker hospital, disease, dangerous occurrence?)
(c) If YES to both: proceed to report.

STEP 3 — REPORT:
(a) Deaths/specified injuries: telephone HSE immediately (0345 300 9923).
(b) All reportable incidents: complete online form within the statutory deadline.
(c) Save/print a copy for the records.

STEP 4 — INVESTIGATE:
(a) Conduct an internal investigation to identify root causes.
(b) Involve health and safety representatives where appointed.
(c) Document findings and corrective actions.
(d) Be prepared for an HSE investigation — cooperate fully.

STEP 5 — IMPLEMENT CORRECTIVE ACTIONS:
(a) Address the root causes to prevent recurrence.
(b) Update risk assessments where necessary.
(c) Communicate lessons learned to the workforce.
(d) Monitor effectiveness of corrective actions.
sections.keyPrinciple: RIDDOR reporting is the employer's FIRST legal obligation after a serious workplace incident. Before investigating, before disciplining, before communicating with insurers — the employer must report. Missing the deadline is a criminal offence. The HR manager should have a laminated RIDDOR decision flowchart at every workplace: What happened? → Is it work-related? → Does it meet a RIDDOR threshold? → Report. This is not bureaucracy — it is a legal obligation that, if breached, becomes an aggravating factor in any subsequent prosecution.
refs: HSE online reporting: www.hse.gov.uk/riddor/report.htm
HSE Incident Contact Centre: 0345 300 9923
HSWA 1974 s.33 (criminal offence — failure to report)
Sentencing Council Health and Safety Offences Guidelines (non-reporting as aggravating factor)
Social Security (Claims and Payments) Regulations 1979 (accident book — separate requirement)
commonErrors: Not establishing an internal reporting system — without a clear process for employees to report ALL incidents to a central point, RIDDOR-qualifying incidents may go undetected until the HSE discovers them through other means

Waiting to see if the employee recovers before reporting — specified injuries must be reported IMMEDIATELY, not after a wait-and-see period. For over-7-day injuries, the 15-day deadline starts from the date of the accident, not the date the 7-day threshold is crossed
Not reporting near-misses (dangerous occurrences) — many employers report injuries but fail to report dangerous occurrences where no injury occurred. These are equally reportable
Conflating the accident book with RIDDOR reporting — they are SEPARATE legal requirements. Recording an incident in the accident book does not discharge the RIDDOR reporting obligation
Not keeping RIDDOR records for 3 years — records must be retained for at least 3 years and produced on request to HSE inspectors

Failing to preserve the scene after a serious incident — the HSE may investigate. Cleaning up, moving equipment, or altering the scene before the HSE has had the opportunity to inspect may constitute obstruction

Not cooperating with HSE investigations following a RIDDOR report — the HSE has extensive powers of entry, inspection, and evidence-gathering under HSWA 1974 ss.20-25. Obstruction is a criminal offence

sections.num: Cross-reference: RIDDOR + HSWA + insurance + civil claims
sections.title: How RIDDOR interacts with employer's liability insurance, civil claims, HSE enforcement, and other notification obligations
sections.summary: A RIDDOR-reportable incident triggers a cascade of interconnected obligations beyond the RIDDOR report itself. The employer must: (a) report under RIDDOR, (b) notify their employer's liability insurer (policy conditions typically require immediate notification of incidents likely to give rise to a claim), (c) conduct an internal investigation under the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999, (d) cooperate with any HSE investigation, (e) review and update the relevant risk assessment, and (f) prepare for the possibility of civil claims for personal injury (which have a 3-year limitation period under the Limitation Act 1980). RIDDOR reports are NOT confidential — they are available to the HSE and may be disclosed in civil proceedings. The content of the report should be FACTUAL and ACCURATE, not defensive or self-serving.
sections.text:
THE RIDDOR CASCADE:

(1) RIDDOR REPORT → HSE is notified → HSE may investigate → HSE may issue improvement/prohibition notice → HSE may prosecute (criminal).

(2) INSURER NOTIFICATION → Employer must notify EL insurer under policy conditions → insurer may appoint loss adjusters → insurer manages the defence.

(3) INTERNAL INVESTIGATION → under MHSWR 1999 Reg.5 → identify root causes → review risk assessments → implement corrective actions.

(4) CIVIL CLAIM (potential) → injured worker may bring personal injury claim → 3-year limitation period → employer and/or insurer defends → RIDDOR report will be disclosed in proceedings.

(5) CORONER'S INQUEST (if death) → RIDDOR report may be before the Coroner → employer may be called as an interested person → separate from criminal proceedings.

RIDDOR REPORT CONTENT — PRACTICAL GUIDANCE:
— Be FACTUAL and ACCURATE. State what happened, when, where, and to whom.
— Do NOT speculate about blame, causation, or liability.
— Do NOT use the report to defend the employer's position.
— Do NOT omit material facts — the report may be compared with witness statements and other evidence.
— The report is a legal document — treat it as such.

INTERACTION WITH ACCIDENT BOOK:
— The accident book (BI 510) is required under Social Security legislation.
— ALL workplace accidents should be recorded in the accident book.
— Only those meeting RIDDOR thresholds need also be reported to the HSE.
— Over-3-day injuries must be RECORDED (accident book) but NOT REPORTED (RIDDOR — threshold is over 7 days).
— The accident book is DATA PROTECTION sensitive — it should be kept securely and entries should be individually removable (modern tear-out page format) to prevent other employees reading each other's entries.
sections.keyPrinciple: A RIDDOR-reportable incident is never just a RIDDOR report. It is the beginning of a multi-strand response that may involve the HSE, insurers, the courts, and (in the case of a death) the Coroner. The HR manager must understand that the RIDDOR report is a LEGAL DOCUMENT that may be scrutinised in criminal proceedings, civil claims, and inquests. It must be factual, complete, and prepared with care.
refs: Employer's Liability (Compulsory Insurance) Act 1969 (EL insurance — mandatory for all employers)
MHSWR 1999 Reg.5 (health and safety arrangements — accident investigation)
Limitation Act 1980 s.11 (3-year limitation period for personal injury claims)
HSWA 1974 ss.20-25 (HSE powers of entry, inspection, and enforcement)
Coroners and Justice Act 2009 (inquests — employer as interested person)
Data Protection Act 2018 (accident book entries contain personal data)
commonErrors: Treating the RIDDOR report as a defensive document — the report should be factual, not argumentative. Defensive or self-serving language will be scrutinised and may undermine the employer's credibility
Not notifying the employer's liability insurer promptly — policy conditions typically require immediate notification. Late notification may prejudice the employer's cover

Not conducting an internal investigation — the MHSWR 1999 require the employer to investigate accidents to identify root causes and prevent recurrence. An employer who reports but does not investigate has missed the point

Not reviewing and updating risk assessments after a RIDDOR incident — the incident is evidence that the existing risk assessment was inadequate or that controls have failed. The risk assessment must be reviewed and updated

Allowing managers to 'coach' witnesses before an HSE investigation — witness statements should be the witness's own account. Coaching or pressuring witnesses may constitute obstruction and will damage the employer's credibility

Not ensuring the accident book complies with data protection — accident book entries contain personal data (names, addresses, injury descriptions). The book must be kept securely, and entries should be in a format that prevents other employees reading each other's records

parts.title: Part 4 — When the Worst Happens: Responding to a Workplace Death

sections.num: Cross-reference: RIDDOR + HSWA + duty of care + human response
sections.title: The first hours after a workplace death — balancing compassion with legal obligation
sections.summary: A workplace death is the most devastating event any employer can face. The HR manager, the business owner, the colleagues who witnessed it — everyone is in shock. In that moment, the employer must simultaneously provide a compassionate human response AND comply with strict legal obligations. These are not competing priorities — they are complementary. The employer who responds with both humanity and professionalism protects both the people affected and the business. This section provides the framework for that dual response: what to do in the first hour, the first day, and the first week.
sections.text:
THE FIRST HOUR — IMMEDIATE RESPONSE:

(1) PROTECT LIFE:
— Call 999 (ambulance, and police if the death involves violence or suspicious circumstances).
— Administer first aid if there is any chance of survival.
— Move other people away from danger.
— Do NOT move the deceased unless doing so is necessary to save another life or remove them from ongoing danger.

(2) SECURE THE SCENE:
— Prevent access to the area where the death occurred.
— Do NOT clean up, move equipment, or alter the scene in any way. The HSE and police will need to examine it.
— If the scene poses an ongoing risk (e.g., unstable structure, live electrical equipment), take only the minimum action necessary to make it safe.

(3) SUPPORT THE PEOPLE:
— Identify anyone who witnessed the death or was directly involved.
— Move witnesses to a quiet, private area. They should not be left alone.
— Do NOT ask witnesses to provide formal statements immediately — they are in shock.
— Contact trained mental health first aiders if available.
— Arrange for a senior manager to be present to provide calm, human leadership.

(4) NOTIFY THE HSE:
— Telephone 0345 300 9923 IMMEDIATELY.
— This is a legal obligation — not optional. The call should be made within the first hour.
— Have ready: the location, the name of the deceased (if known), a brief description of what happened.
— The HSE may dispatch an inspector immediately.

(5) NOTIFY THE POLICE:
— If the death may involve criminal negligence, the police may investigate alongside the HSE.
— In some cases, the police will attend automatically (e.g., if called by ambulance crew).
— The employer should cooperate fully but should not make admissions or speculate about blame.

THE FIRST DAY — STRUCTURED RESPONSE:

(1) NOTIFY THE FAMILY:
— The family should be told IN PERSON, not by telephone, wherever possible.
— The most senior person available should deliver the news, accompanied by a trained support person.
— What to say: the facts of what happened, as far as they are known. Express sincere condolences.
— What NOT to say: do not speculate about blame, cause, or fault. Do not make promises about investigations or outcomes.
— Offer practical support: the family may need help with transport, contacting other relatives, or understanding what happens next.
— Assign a single point of contact within the organisation for the family — someone with the authority and sensitivity to handle ongoing communication.

(2) SUPPORT THE WORKFORCE:
— Gather the workforce (or the affected team/department) and tell them what has happened. Be honest about what is known and what is not yet known.
— Acknowledge the shock and grief. It is appropriate for the employer to express emotion.
— Send affected employees home if they wish to leave. Do not pressure anyone to continue working.
— Arrange access to counselling and mental health support — many employers have Employee Assistance Programmes (EAPs) that provide immediate telephone counselling.
— Be aware that the impact may be delayed — some employees will seem fine on day one and collapse on day three.
— WITNESSES and FIRST RESPONDERS are at particular risk of post-traumatic stress. They should be offered specialist trauma support, not just general counselling.

(3) INSTRUCT SPECIALIST SOLICITORS:
— Instruct specialist health and safety solicitors IMMEDIATELY — ideally the same day.
— Anything the employer says to the HSE, the police, or in the RIDDOR report is EVIDENTIAL and may be used in criminal proceedings.
— Legal privilege protects communications between the employer and their solicitor — but ONLY if the solicitor is instructed.
— The solicitor will advise on: the RIDDOR report wording, the HSE investigation process, interview rights, document preservation, and insurance notification.

(4) NOTIFY THE INSURER:
— The employer's liability insurer must be notified immediately under the policy conditions.
— The insurer will typically appoint loss adjusters and may provide legal support.
— Late notification may prejudice the employer's cover.

THE FIRST WEEK — INVESTIGATION AND CARE:

(1) HSE INVESTIGATION:
— An HSE inspector will attend the scene, often within hours.
— The inspector has powers to: enter the premises, take photographs and measurements, seize equipment and documents, take witness statements, and require the employer to answer questions.
— The employer (and any individual manager) has the right to be accompanied by a solicitor during any interview.
— The employer should cooperate fully but should not volunteer information beyond what is asked.
— CAUTION: anything said by the employer or individual managers may be used in a criminal prosecution. Do NOT make admissions, speculate about blame, or try to 'explain' what happened. Answer questions factually.

(2) CORONER'S PROCESS:
— All workplace deaths are reported to the Coroner (England and Wales) or Procurator Fiscal (Scotland).
— The Coroner will order a post-mortem examination.
— An inquest will be held — this is a fact-finding inquiry, not a criminal trial.
— The employer is usually designated an 'interested person' and has the right to attend the inquest, ask questions of witnesses, and be represented by a lawyer.
— The Coroner may record a verdict of: accidental death, unlawful killing, narrative verdict, or other conclusions.
— The inquest may take place months or years after the death.
— A Regulation 28 'prevention of future deaths' report may be issued if the Coroner believes there is a risk of further deaths. The employer must respond within 56 days.

(3) ONGOING WORKFORCE SUPPORT:
— Grief does not follow a timetable. The employer should maintain support services for weeks and months, not just days.
— Consider holding a memorial or tribute — this can be healing for the workforce.
— Be sensitive about returning to work at the location where the death occurred — some employees may find this extremely difficult.
— Monitor for signs of post-traumatic stress in witnesses and first responders.
— If the deceased had a close working partner or team, that team may need restructuring with sensitivity.

(4) COMPLETING THE RIDDOR REPORT:
— The written online report must be submitted within 10 days of the death.
— The report should be prepared with input from the employer's solicitor.
— It should be FACTUAL — date, time, location, brief description of what happened, identity of the deceased.
— It should NOT contain admissions, opinions about causation, or defensive language.
— Keep a copy for the employer's records (3-year minimum).
sections.keyPrinciple: The worst day of a business owner's professional life is not the time to discover their legal obligations. Eileen's role is to provide the framework BEFORE it is needed, and to be there to guide WHEN it is needed. The employer who responds to a workplace death with both humanity and legal precision — who cares for the people AND preserves the legal position — is the employer who survives this crisis. Compassion and compliance are not in tension. They are the same response expressed through different actions.
refs: RIDDOR 2013 Reg.6(1) (immediate notification of death)
HSWA 1974 s.2 (general duty of care to employees)
HSWA 1974 ss.20-25 (HSE enforcement powers)
Coroners and Justice Act 2009 (inquest process)
Coroner's Regulation 28 (prevention of future deaths reports)
Employer's Liability (Compulsory Insurance) Act 1969
Legal privilege — Police and Criminal Evidence Act 1984 s.10
Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 Reg.5 (accident investigation)
commonErrors: Not calling the HSE because everyone is focused on the immediate human crisis — the HSE notification (0345 300 9923) must happen within the first hour. It is a brief factual call, not a detailed report. Designate one person to make this call while others manage the human response

Moving or cleaning the scene before the HSE has inspected it — this may constitute obstruction and destroys evidence. The scene must be preserved as far as possible. Only the minimum action needed to protect safety should be taken

Notifying the family by telephone rather than in person — wherever practically possible, a senior person from the organisation should deliver the news face to face. This is not a legal requirement but it is a moral one. The way the employer treats the family in the first hours will define the relationship for years

Not instructing specialist solicitors immediately — by the time the HSE inspector arrives (often within hours), the employer should have legal advice. Anything said to the HSE is evidential. Without legal advice, employers and managers may make admissions that cannot be retracted

Pressuring witnesses to provide formal statements while they are still in shock — witnesses should be given time to recover before providing statements. Rushing this process produces unreliable accounts and harms the witnesses' wellbeing

Not providing ongoing mental health support beyond the first week — grief and trauma are not resolved in a week. Witnesses and first responders may develop PTSD weeks or months later. The employer's duty of care continues
Treating the Coroner's inquest as a formality — the inquest is a judicial proceeding. The employer should be legally represented. A Regulation 28 report can have serious consequences for the business

Not considering whether the location where the death occurred can be sensitively returned to use — asking employees to return to the exact spot where a colleague died, without acknowledgment or support, is a failure of care that may itself cause psychological harm

pendingAmendments.status: Monitor HSE annual enforcement statistics for trends in RIDDOR non-compliance prosecutions and penalty levels. The Sentencing Council guidelines (2016) have increased fine levels significantly for organisations.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — fine levels have increased substantially since the 2016 Sentencing Guidelines
